title: UA pays $150,000 fall claim
summary: UA pays $150,000 fall claim By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter The University of Alaska has paid $150,000 to settle the second of an estimated half-dozen claims resulting from last summer's Ptarmigan Peak accident that killed two climbing students and injured 12 others, including the two instructors. The check was written to 17-year-old Kirsten Staveland. But $5,000 of the settlement will go to her 12-year-old brother, who suffered emotional distress while waiting to learn his sister's fate on the day of the accident, according to a two-page settlement agreement. Late last month, the university settled with student Juanita Palmer Littlefield for $270,000. No other claims have been settled, university officials said. "It's unfortunate we had to go this route," said Kristen's father, Ch…

title: Kenai red run slow to appear
summary: Kenai red run slow to appear Fishermen fear crash The Associated Press KENAI - Despite fishermen's worries, state fish biologists say there's no reason - yet - to think the Kenai River red salmon run will be a disaster like the one in Bristol Bay. "The run of reds is a little late, but it's not time to panic yet," said Paul Reusch of the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. His opinion was seconded by Kelly Hepler, Southcentral management biologist with the department's sportfish division. "There is no indication, so far, that the Kenai run is in trouble. The fish just haven't shown up yet," Hepler said. But Theo Matthews, president of the United Fishermen of Alaska, said he was worried. "That's what they said about Bristol Bay two weeks ago," Matthews said. The red run is about a week behi…

title: Kenai search ends at overturned ATV
summary: Kenai search ends at overturned ATV By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau After an exhausting weekend combing the woods around the city of Kenai, searchers on Tuesday found the body of Ben Copple beneath his overturned four-wheeler. It appeared that Copple, 40, was driving his new 600cc Yamaha Grizzly on a 45-degree embankment bordering the Kenai Spur Highway when the 640-pound machine tipped over, according to a preliminary investigation by Kenai police. He died as a result, and no foul play is suspected, said police Lt. Jeff Kohler. "The machine was resting on top of him," Kohler said. Copple and his two brothers had a lot of friends, many of whom turned out to aid a massive air and land search that began Saturday. A few of those volunteers located the body at 10:50 a.m. near Mile 4 …

title: Dozens scour Kenai area for missing man
summary: Dozens scour Kenai area for missing man By RACHEL D'ORO Daily News reporter Ben Copple is rarely late for work, and he never calls in sick. So when the Kenai man didn't show up for work after a planned few hours of fishing, his worried brothers launched a massive air and ground search that entered its third frustrating day Monday. Copple, 40, was due at work Friday afternoon at ERA Aviation's Kenai office, but he never arrived. "That is extremely uncommon for him," Matt Copple said of his older brother. "We had to do something." Led by Matt Copple and another brother, an exhausted team of volunteers combed the banks of the Kenai River just south of the missing man's trailer home, the marshes and lakes to the north and an intricate web of trails throughout the area. Copple spends much of hi…

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Bristol Bay bust Double-whammy salmon flop sends shock waves far, wide By HELEN JUNG Daily News reporter NAKNEK - Violet Willson on Friday afternoon was desperately figuring out how the earnings of a six-hour fishing season can cover a year's worth of needs. With this summer's Bristol Bay red salmon fishery likely to end with the worst catch in two decades, Willson, 64, is feeling the stress set in. She's waited days for the salmon to surge into the bay. Her nerves have frayed, and her hope has worn thin. The fish aren't coming, she acknowledged, and she and her family face the second year in a row of dealing with disaster. "This is the only source of income, and without it we have nothing," she said. "And I have never in my life depended on welfare." Last year, when the Bristol Bay run ca…

title: Hiker falls to his death in state park
summary: Hiker falls to his death in state park By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter A 21-year-old man hiking with friends in the rugged mountains above Eklutna Lake fell to his death late Friday afternoon, authorities said. The name of the man was not released Friday night because his relatives had not been notified. The man spent summers in Anchorage and winters in East Lansing, Mich., said Frank Wesser, a Chugach State Park ranger. The man fell while scrambling on steep rocky terrain high on East Twin Peak north of Eklutna Lake, Wesser said. The man had climbed on ahead of his friends, a man and a woman, and out of their sight. They heard him cry out and heard rocks falling. An Alaska Air National Guard helicopter dropped off rescuers, who descended to the site Friday evening and found the man's b…

title: Bristol Bay faces another disaster
summary: Bristol Bay faces another disaster By HELEN JUNG Daily News reporter Bristol Bay salmon harvest, 1993 to 1998. NAKNEK - The state's most valuable salmon fishery is likely to end with the lowest catch in two decades, sounding alarms that for the second year in a row the Bristol Bay region is facing economic disaster. Nearly a third of the 2,800 fishing-permit holders are area residents, many of whom rely on their catches as their main source of income. In addition, the local governments rely on fish-tax revenue to fund services from police to road maintenance. Town officials said Thursday that, like last year, they will have to figure out how to cope with millions less in revenue. Fish processors are preparing to close some of their plants and send hundreds of workers packing with little ca…

title: Y-K salmon runs at 'disaster' mark
summary: Y-K salmon runs at 'disaster' mark By TOM KIZZIA Daily News reporter Western Alaska is facing economic disaster this summer, state officials said Wednesday, as poor salmon returns spread from Bristol Bay to the Yukon River. The worst king salmon run of the century on the Yukon has all but stopped commercial fishing in villages with little other economic base. Yukon chum runs appear to be as bad as the disastrous returns of 1993, according to the Department of Fish and Game. On the Kuskokwim River, chum returns are a little better than last year, when they were so bad subsistence was interrupted and minimum spawning goals to ensure future returns weren't reached. The Kuskokwim chum catch is expected to be two-thirds below normal. And in Bristol Bay, the state's most valuable salmon fishery,…

title: 'Just glad to be fishing'
summary: 'Just glad to be fishing' Boats blitz river for scant reds By HELEN JUNG Daily News reporter ABOARD THE F/V ECOLA ANNE - More than 70 boats crammed the water in front of him, but Cliff Lewis jammed down the red throttle of his 32-foot boat and raced for the front line. Forcing his way among the crowd, Lewis staked out a place just shy of the invisible boundary that divides legal from illegal fishing. His crew spun out 300 feet of net into the water and waited. Somehow in the muddy Naknek River, 175 red salmon skirted the dozens of gillnets streaming behind the other boats to head straight for the F/V Ecola Anne. Ten minutes later, his crew reeled in the fish - his best catch so far of the one-hour-old season. The catch wasn't great by the standards of past years, and fishing would drop off…

title: 'Japanese Caribou' makes goal
summary: 'Japanese Caribou' makes goal Trek to Arctic Ocean ends By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter With almost 900 miles of walking behind him, the self-dubbed "Japanese Caribou" wet his hand in the Arctic Ocean on Monday evening. Masatoshi Kuriaki, who ascended Mount McKinley alone in late winter and began walking north from Anchorage in early April, reached Deadhorse at the terminus of the Dalton Highway on Sunday evening, he said from an Arco Alaska security office at the entrance to Prudhoe Bay. Kuriaki said he received a "special walking permit" from Arco that allowed him to cross the oil field and walk seven miles to the shore of the Beaufort Sea. "Today my walking all done," he said Monday. "Last evening I got to Deadhorse, but didn't yet (finish), but today my walking all finish." Oil fiel…

title: At long last, Bristol Bay seeing reds
summary: At long last, Bristol Bay seeing reds But fish woes still not over By HELEN JUNG Daily News reporter NAKNEK - At 8 p.m. Sunday, commercial fishermen and seafood processors across Bristol Bay huddled around radios and joined in a collective hush. Over the airwaves came word from the Alaska Department of Fish and Game that the red salmon run, which had been stalling all summer long, was showing some signs of revving up. Still, Bristol Bay again might be facing one of the most dismal salmon returns in the past 20 years, compounding the catastrophe of last year's poor run. Typically, the salmon return to Bristol Bay would have peaked by now, bringing millions of dollars in revenue to the thousands of fishermen, processing workers and seafood companies that operate in Alaska's most valuable sal…

title: Brits get wild ride off McKinley
summary: Brits get wild ride off McKinley High altitude, dangling brings down pair in good shape By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter Dangling from a line 100 feet below a helicopter, two British mountain climbers stranded since Friday on a steep slab of ice at the 19,000-foot level of Mount McKinley were lifted to safety early Monday morning. J.D. Swed, south district ranger with the National Park Service, said Lama rescue helicopter pilot Jim Hood took advantage of a window of calm weather on the mountain to lower the climbers a pair of body harnesses called "screamer suits." Before he could retrieve the climbers, Hood landed lower on the mountain to "get a sip of fuel," Swed said. Hood had to fly with minimal fuel. It's hard for helicopters to operate in thin air at high altitudes, and weight is cr…

title: Thefts rattle Kenai miners
summary: Thefts rattle Kenai miners 7 dredges stolen from camps between Hope, Summit Lake By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Thieves who crave gold-mining dredges struck the mother lode last month, stealing equipment and trashing the camps of about a half-dozen miners in the heart of the Kenai Mountains. Angry miners say they've lost tens of thousands of dollars and want to post a $1,000 reward for tips leading to the arrest of the thieves. "I'm having a hard time sleeping at night now," said Brian Berkhahn of Anchorage, whose Hope-area camp was intact the last time he checked it last week. Bill and Glenna Dunlevy weren't so lucky. In late November, Bill Dunlevy hiked back to his remote claim on Gulch Creek, not far from the new Seward Highway bridge at the Hope cutoff, only to fi…

title: 4 climbers stranded after falls on Mount McKinley
summary: 4 climbers stranded after falls on Mount McKinley By MAUREEN CLARK The Associated Press Rescue crews were trying Friday to rescue four climbers, including two Alaskans, injured in two falls in a treacherous snow chute high on Mount McKinley. But weather was keeping high-altitude helicopters on the ground. Three British army climbers fell 300 feet Thursday evening, while two Alaskans fell nearly 3,000 feet early Friday, National Park Service spokeswoman Jane Tranel said. Both Alaskans suffered serious injuries in the fall. Jeff Munroe, 25, of Anchorage was unconscious and in critical condition with a head injury. Billy Finley, 24, also of Anchorage, had chest injuries and fractured ribs, as well as ankle and knee injuries. They were brought down from the base of the chute at about 15,500 fe…

title: Alcohol controls sought
summary: Alcohol controls sought Board wants village distribution centers By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter The rules that residents in Alaska's 14 damp communities follow to buy alcohol might be tightened this summer. The Alcoholic Beverage Control Board is proposing a new system that would require people to pick up their booze orders from a government center instead of at the airport as they have for years. Employees at the centers - likely a clerk's office in a small village or a warehouse in a larger town like Bethel - will document all liquor shipments coming into the community and note who has ordered them. Each village will have the option of charging a handling fee to cover administration costs. Liquor board officials expect regulations making the change to be completed later this wi…

title: Clinton spawns theory
summary: Clinton spawns theory Salmon war linked to global warming By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - President Clinton offered a novel explanation Tuesday for the continuing salmon war between U.S. and Canadian commercial fishermen: global warming. "I can tell you, one of my big problems with our best partner in the world, Canada, is that our salmon fishermen are fighting all the time," Clinton said in a speech at a Democratic National Committee fund-raiser. "You know why? Because all the salmon are moving north, so there are more in Alaska and fewer in Canada, because of climate change." If true, that would be hot news. It appears, however, that no one except maybe the president believes it. British Columbia fishermen complain that Alaska fishermen intercept too many salmo…

title: Success floods hospital
summary: Success floods hospital Native admissions put strain on budget By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter Since the Alaska Native Medical Center moved to state-of-the-art quarters last year, the number of patients seeking medical help there has grown beyond expectations. Outpatient visits, mainly to clinic doctors, have risen by more than 35 percent. Surgeries for outpatients have grown a like amount. The number of patients admitted for care has jumped more than 10 percent, compared with that of the year before the hospital opened. The startling demand has created budget headaches as a new Native-led team gears up to take over the hospital from the U.S. Indian Health Service. Hospital administrators and Native health leaders have worked the past two months to get spending in line before Jan. 1. Tha…

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Ridge turns killer Wind, ice mold a McKinley trap By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter A month of high winds and slippery footing has created a new zone of death and disaster on Mount McKinley. Along a relatively small section of the West Buttress ridge, three climbers have fallen in two weeks, resulting in one confirmed fatality, one man missing and presumed dead, and a desperate search for the third. The latest casualty, 21-year-old Seattle mountain guide Chris Hooyman, lost his balance late Saturday morning while descending the buttress ridge at about the 17,000-foot level and fell down the north slope toward the Peters Glacier, the National Park Service said. Heavy snow and high winds kept rescuers pinned down Sunday, but a pair of climbers took advantage of a break in the weather early …

title: Hare boom defies easy explanation
summary: Hare boom defies easy explanation By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter There's a rabbit explosion in Anchorage. Technically, they are snowshoe hares. They are showing up everywhere. Dead on the side of the road. Scooting across the lawn in the morning. Munching in the garden at night. The Anchorage area has hit, or is nearing, the high in the 10- to 12-year cycle of snowshoe hare boom-bust cycles, according to biologists. "You know you are at the high numbers when you see them all over the road," said Tony Sinclair, an ecology professor at the University of British Columbia. Sinclair and a team of scientists specialize in studying the snowshoe hares' population cycle. "You only see them everywhere at the peak," Sinclair said. It will be over soon, he added. "They don't last more than tw…

title: 15 flee Interior wildfire
summary: 15 flee Interior wildfire Carla Lake blaze leaps fire lines By MAUREEN CLARK The Associated Press A wildfire burning in the Interior flared up Monday, jumping beyond containment lines and prompting the evacuation of about 15 people from recreational cabins. The 45,000-acre Carla Lake fire was fanned by winds gusting to 50 mph. It was threatening about 33 cabins on Clear Creek and the south bank of the Tanana River, said John See, a fire management officer with the state Division of Forestry. Most of the cabins are used only on weekends, and only about 10 of the cabins were occupied when the evacuation order was issued. "The operations folks, along with state troopers, have removed the residents out of the area," said fire information officer Roger Stilipec. "The owners that I've talked to …

title: Storms bedevil search
summary: Storms bedevil search 3rd climbing fatality feared on McKinley By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter Rescuers were forced to give up the search for a missing Mount McKinley climbing guide Sunday as the mountain was belted with 50 to 100 miles per hour winds and intense snowfall. "No one is moving on the mountain," said Jane Tranel, a Denali National Park and Preserve spokeswoman. About 500 climbers on the mountain were hunkered down Sunday in tents and snowcaves. "Everyone is in survival mode." The search for Chris Hooyman of Seattle will resume when weather permits, she added. "But the forecast is bleak." The 21-year-old Hooyman, who is from Seattle and is an assistant guide for the Tacoma-based Rainier Mountaineering Inc., was climbing down the mountain with two other guides around 11 …

title: Pet owner dies after argument
summary: Pet owner dies after argument By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter A 60-year-old Girdwood man walking his dog without a leash died of a heart attack after a "very aggressive verbal altercation" with an Animal Control officer, an Alaska state trooper said Friday. Ted Johnson, a member of the Girdwood Board of Supervisors, struck the officer with a walking stick Thursday, knocking her to the ground, trooper Sgt. Lee Oly said. He later collapsed at the scene. The officer, Kimberly Ann Martens, had run off after being struck, and then joined a neighbor in an attempt to revive him while waiting for paramedics to arrive, Oly said. Johnson was pronounced dead about an hour later. Martens "was devastated by the whole thing," Oly said. "The poor animal control officer was as much a victim as he was."…

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Blaze ruled arson Beachfront fire cause known By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter The fire that destroyed a six-plex in the Beachfront Apartments early Thursday morning was started intentionally and police are investigating the related death of a man as a homicide, investigators said Friday. But authorities weren't saying much more about the blaze that also displaced six families and put a woman into a Seattle hospital with critical burns. Jacqueline Robinson ran in flames from the apartment where the fire began shortly before 5 a.m. Thursday. She suffered burns over 80 percent of her body. She was flown to Harborview Medical Center in Seattle, where she remained in critical condition Friday night, a nursing supervisor said. Anchorage Fire Department spokeswoman Kayle Foster said arson inves…

title: Lindauer offers to pay, settle case
summary: Lindauer offers to pay, settle case By RICHARD MAUER Daily News reporter John Lindauer says he is ready to settle the Alaska Public Offices Commission disclosure case against him by paying a $1,460 fine, though he continues to deny that he violated the law. With the three attorneys hired by him and his wife costing far more than the maximum $10-a-day civil fine he'd face if he lost, Lindauer told APOC he'd rather pay the penalty and get it over with. Lindauer, who received less than 18 percent of the vote in the November election as the Republican nominee for governor, made the offer at the end of a 25-page legal response he filed last Friday with APOC. The document was in answer to an APOC complaint charging him with failing to disclose business interests and financial activity as require…

title: School workers vote to back strike
summary: School workers vote to back strike By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter School secretaries and teacher aides have voted 599 to 87 to reject a proposed contract with the school district and support a strike, the Totem Association announced Wednesday. Totem president Ellen Gamel said members fear they'll lose money over the three-year term of the contract because the Anchorage School District's contributions to health insurance, though increasing, won't keep pace with costs. "They want to break even. They don't want to be behind," Gamel said. She said nearly 700 of 900-plus Totem members met at East High on Tuesday night and voted on the tentative agreement that union and district negotiators reached in mid-November. The union represents clerical workers, teacher assistants, secretaries…

title: Fisheries managers worry over fewer licensed anglers
summary: Fisheries managers worry over fewer licensed anglers By CRAIG MEDRED Daily News outdoors editor For the first time in a decade, sportfishing license sales in Alaska have taken a significant drop, forcing fishery managers to cut their budget by almost $2 million. Sales were lagging 12 percent behind last year in financial reports at the end of November, but state officials expect that by the time late-season license sales trickle in from vendors they will be within 4 percent of last year's sales. Most hope the decline is an anomaly, but sport fisheries director Kevin Delaney fears this could be a sign Alaska is starting to follow a national decline in sportfishing. With the exception of the Kenai River, plagued by a weak king salmon run this year, guides, charter boat operators and others r…

title: Sound studies at odds
summary: Sound studies at odds New work puts Exxon on hook By BEN SPIESS Daily News reporter A new study counters a long-held claim by Exxon that oil seeping from the ground is a natural part of the Prince William Sound environment. Discounting natural oil as a contaminant in the Sound, the new work suggests that signs of oil pollution in sea otters and ducks is due to the 1989 spill. "It puts Exxon back on the hook," said Bob Spies, chief scientist for the Exxon Valdez Oil Spill Trustee Council, the group that funded the study. The new study is a step toward answering one of the big questions about Prince William Sound: Where have high levels of chemical compounds in the sea floor come from? A scientific study funded by Exxon reported in 1996 that the compounds, known as hydrocarbons, come from na…

title: Airport pipeline workers damage CEA electric cable
summary: Airport pipeline workers damage CEA electric cable By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter Workers installing a new jet-fuel pipeline from the Port of Anchorage to Anchorage International Airport sliced into a high-voltage electrical cable, construction and utility officials said Monday. The cable, a spare not carrying electricity, was broken about 3 p.m. Friday, and no customers lost power, said Lee Thibert, executive manager of transmission and distribution services for Chugach Electric Association. The jacket of a second cable, which carried power, was slightly damaged by a Conam Construction Co., backhoe operator. "Everybody maybe saw a blip in their power," Thibert said. It will take up to three months, however, to finish splicing in a new section of the cut cable, and only the cable's Eur…

title: Escaped mental patient back in Anchorage court
summary: Escaped mental patient back in Anchorage court By RACHEL D'ORO Daily News reporter Frank A. Alto, the killer and mental patient who ran away to New York, is back in Alaska. Alto, 45, returned to Anchorage late Sunday, nearly a month after he escaped custody during a field trip with the Alaska Psychiatric Institute, where he's been a patient for 21 years. He was arraigned Monday in District Court on one count of second-degree escape, a felony that carries a sentence of up to 10 years in prison. Alto was convicted in 1974 of the rape and murder of a family friend. In 1977, the state Supreme Court overturned his conviction, finding him not guilty by reason of insanity, and remanded him to API, the state's largest psychiatric facility. On Nov. 5, Alto walked away from a coffee shop near Dimond…

title: Firearm checks sluggish
summary: Firearm checks sluggish 'Instant' system off to slow start By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter A new "instant" background check of gun buyers that went into effect Monday across the nation wasn't working very well in Anchorage, unless having to dial an FBI hot line 20 times to get past a busy signal qualifies as "instant." Even then, said salesmen at Great Northern Guns on Tudor Road, two of three checks they ran on customers elicited a "delayed" response from operators at an 800 number on the East Coast. That meant the customer had to go away for up to three days before the sale could be completed. "They're still using both hands to find their backside," said Great Northern salesman Jeff Nowak. Called "permanent Brady" by federal enforcers, the new criminal background check expands the 1…

title: Delta Junction considering new vote on Greely prison
summary: Delta Junction considering new vote on Greely prison The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - A plan that would create a medium-security private prison at Fort Greely could come up for another public vote in December. The Delta Junction City Council wants more public input before making a final decision on the project. The prison proposal would place an 800-bed prison at the Army base, which is being downsized by the Pentagon. Allvest Inc., the Anchorage-based halfway house company that proposed the prison project, would like to have the facility up and running by January 2000. City clerk Pam Ellis said the council may talk today about scheduling an election, although the matter was not on its meeting agenda. Delta Junction residents have voted once on the project. An advisory vote on the prison p…

title: Alaska's role key to Arctic Council
summary: Alaska's role key to Arctic Council By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - Alaska's prominence in northern health and technology could rise dramatically over the next two years as the United States takes charge of a fledgling panel of eight arctic nations in search of solutions to common problems. Just 2 years old, the Arctic Council is an amorphous fusion of science and diplomacy that evolved out of concern over staggering pollution problems in Russia after the collapse of the Soviet Union. Russia pollution remains the council's top priority. But it has moved into other areas and now is delving into the effects of global climate change on arctic nations and ways to build sustainable local economies. Health professionals, environmentalists, Native leaders and others in …

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: New dome Parishioners leave St. Nicholas of Myra Byzantine Catholic Church Saturday night after vespers, during which the newest cupola, far left, was unveiled and lit. The domes, lit from the inside, are the only ones of this type in the United States. The church is on Arctic Boulevard in Midtown. (JIM LAVRAKAS / Anchorage Daily News) Back | Top | Home | User Agreement Problems? Suggestions? Let us hear from you. Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News

title: Millions of teens depressed
summary: Millions of teens depressed Depression rampant in teens By STEVE FAINARU The Boston Globe Rates of clinical depression among teenagers are as high as they have ever been, and the recent spate of multiple murders in American schools has led to nationwide efforts to detect children at risk for violence and suicide, which also has risen dramatically in recent years. The National Institute of Mental Health in Washington estimates that 1.5 million children younger than 18 suffer from serious depression, a treatable mood disorder. Other studies put the numbers much higher. "To me, it's one of the saddest things in our society, that we're not doing anything about this," said Kevin Dwyer, president-elect for the National Association of School Psychologists. "If we don't address the needs of these …

title: Teen computer whiz ahead of the program
summary: Teen computer whiz ahead of the program By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter Chris Granade began writing computer programs using the language called BASIC when he was 7, about the time many children are reading "Frog and Toad" books. Last week, at 13, Chris passed an exam that would qualify him for a well-paying computer job - if he were old enough. He is a Microsoft Certified Professional in Visual Basic. "It's a pretty big deal," said Kelly O'Conner of Network Business Systems, the Microsoft testing center in Anchorage where Chris qualified. "We were very impressed." Most of those certified are at least high school graduates and many work in a technical field, O'Conner said. Only 236,000 people nationwide are Microsoft-certified professionals, according to a Microsoft web site. As a…

title: Scientists argue cause of underwater changes
summary: Scientists argue cause of underwater changes By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter FAIRBANKS - Something is changing in the North Pacific. A wide band of algae, picked up by satellites because it has turned the normally dark water an aqua blue, is blooming in the Bering Sea. A biologist working in the area spotted a pod of five to seven right whales, an endangered species that hasn't been seen in those numbers in nearly a century. And for the first time since the 1940s, Canadian commercial fishermen are harvesting sardines. No one knows whether the changes can be tied simply to El Nino, the phenomenon that warmed much of the Pacific Ocean over the past two years, or is the result of something even bigger. Researchers are trying to find out. Nearly 300 scientists from countries rimming th…

title: Home run race gave us something different, and better, to watch
summary: Home run race gave us something different, and better, to watch By MIKE DOOGAN I was moving the ladder Sunday when the woman who lets me live with her opened the living room window. "McGwire just hit his 70th, with two men on," she said. I figured she was just jerking my chain. Here I was, a lifelong baseball fan, dutifully doing fall chores. There she was, a woman who would rather have a root canal than watch sports, camped in front of the TV. There is no justice. "Sure he did," I snarled. "He hit it with one hand, and the ball left St. Louis completely." "No, really, he did," she said, before cranking the window shut. "If you just climb the ladder, you can see for yourself." I climbed the ladder and looked through the window. There was McGwire exchanging high fives with his teammates. Th…

title: Miss Alaska joins pageant's top 10
summary: Miss Alaska joins pageant's top 10 By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter First she won the swimsuit competition, a small miracle for a woman from the coldest state in the nation. Then, on Saturday, she became one of 10 finalists for the title Miss America 1999. But, alas, there was no crown for Miss Alaska Joslyn Tinker on Saturday night. That honor went to Miss Virginia Nicole Johnson, a 24-year-old diabetic who wears an insulin pump like a pager on her hip. Tinker "did very well," said Mark Glover, executive director of the Miss Alaska Scholarship Program, from the pageant in Atlantic City, N.J. "We're very proud of her." So were the people watching TV Saturday night at J-Bar-B Bar and Restaurant in Tinker's hometown of Soldotna. The tape-delayed show was broadcast at 9 p.m. Alaska ti…

title: UA settles with another victim of fall
summary: UA settles with another victim of fall By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter The University of Alaska has reached a $150,000 settlement in the third of an estimated half-dozen claims following last year's fatal climbing accident on Ptarmigan Peak. The settlement was reached with 17-year-old Jerilyn Pomeroy, one of the injured climbers. The first settlement was reached in June with injured climbing student Juanita Palmer Littlefield, 44, for $270,000. In July, a settlement was reached with 17-year-old student Kirtsen Staveland for $150,000. No lawsuits have been filed against the university, but attorneys for six of the climbing students and the families of the two who died sent the university letters stating that they intended to pursue claims for medical bills, losses, and pain and suff…

title: Letters to the Daily News
summary: Letters to the Daily News Thanks to Donley for park aid The July 31 Compass piece by Arliss Sturgulewski and George Sullivan outlining the development of a park in Midtown south of the Loussac Library was informative and described the need for additional parkland very well. In addition to the praise given the many Midtown Park volunteers, Mayor Mystrom and city officials, Sen. Dave Donley deserves thanks for his hard work in Juneau to secure the state funding that made those land acquisitions possible. Sen. Donley represents Midtown Anchorage and was the leader who successfully secured over $250,000 in 1996 and another $344,000 in 1997 to further develop and expand the Midtown Park. State funds obtained in 1996 and 1997, thanks to Sen. Donley, were supplemented with local funds from munici…

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Hunter in hot water Bungled plan wrecks machine By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter John Atkins' dead moose was at the bottom of a ravine in Chugach State Park. So he got on a nearby piece of heavy equipment parked overnight by an Anchorage tree service and tried to maneuver it to retrieve his kill, authorities said. Atkins ended up in the hospital after the 11-ton machine rolled down the steep embankment. Atkins, of Indian, will be cited for using a motorized vehicle in a nonmotorized area of the park, ranger Bruce Bigelow said Thursday. He added that he would wait until Atkins was well enough to talk to slap him with the $200 citation. Atkins, 42, asked that information about his injuries not be released, said a nursing supervisor at Providence Alaska Medical Center. The accident also cau…
